key_observations: Significant 6-week volume drop (16.9km → 6.3km) suggests planned deload or injury recovery, followed by recovery week (Jun 22: 15.7km) indicating return to training
HR elevation during recovery phase (158→163 bpm) with maintained pace (5:50-6:10/km) suggests cardiovascular adaptation and improved efficiency post-taper
Low frequency pattern (1 run per week May 25-Jun 8) is concerning for maintenance of aerobic base; recent uptick to 3-4 runs/week (Jun 22+) shows positive momentum
warnings: 2-week data gaps (May 13-25, Jun 10-22) limit trend analysis; cross-reference with any injury notes or structured training blocks
Acute-chronic ratio of 1.18 is acceptable but approaching caution threshold (>1.5); monitor if volume increases further
Football activity (Jun 27) shows elevated HR (158 avg, 188 max); ensure adequate recovery before harder running sessions
recommendations: Continue building run frequency to 4-5x/week; current 2-3 weekly runs insufficient for aerobic base maintenance. Prioritize easy runs at 6:15-6:30/km pace
Establish consistent HR zones with recent data; avg HR trending upward (158→163) while pace holds suggests need for structured threshold/tempo work to prevent compensatory recruiting
Schedule 1 easy week every 4 weeks after reaching 25-30 km/week to manage fatigue and prevent overtraining given moderate fatigue signals

weeks_analysis.assessment: Solid base-building week with 2 runs; 6:06-6:14 pace indicates steady aerobic work
Single 13.7km run suggests planned recovery or scheduling constraint; pace consistent
Marked volume decline (9.5km, 1 run); likely injury recovery or structured taper initiated
Lowest volume week (7.2km); continued deload phase with maintained 6:12/km suggests conservative return
Minimal volume week (6.3km, 1 run); taper nadir. Pace quickens to 5:47/km—possible readiness indicator
Strong return week: 3 runs, 15.7km, avg HR 158 at 5:50/km pace signals successful recovery and good fitness
Post-recovery taper week (6.8km, 1 run); HR rises 5 bpm despite similar pace—likely fatigue accumulation; appropriate caution
